scope.summary: Author cross-repo-evidence-contract-v1: four versioned JSON evidence schemas, negative fixtures, and an offline stdlib-only validator.
out_of_scope: Producing real proofs: the commands that emit these documents are f1cl.2, f1cl.3 and f1cl.7.
Wiring scripts/check_evidence_contract.sh into a CI workflow: no workflow YAML was changed.
Enforcing which changes must have an entry point (f1cl.6 owns the tri-state) and branch policy (f1cl.3), both left as declared seams.

entry_points.name: scripts/check_evidence_contract.sh
entry_points.trace: Runs the 19-fixture suite; exits non-zero when any rule stops firing or a shared $def drifts. Verified exit 0 with output, and each of the 14 negative fixtures asserted on exact code AND path.

entry_points.name: scripts/validate_evidence.py FILE.json
entry_points.trace: Validates a single document; --json emits findings for machine consumers.

limitations: readiness.basis is scoped-test, so this close cannot claim ready: the Required graph (clippy, workspace test) was NOT run. The diff contains zero Rust and the shared checkout held another pane's in-flight Rust edits, so a workspace run would have reported on their tree rather than this change. No green is claimed for it.
No required-proof/v1 document is referenced because no producer exists yet; f1cl.2 builds it. Until then nothing in this repo can honestly claim readiness.basis=required-proof.
The semantic layer is re-implemented per repo; only the schema files and rule codes are shared, so the two implementations can drift in message text (the codes are the contract, not the messages).

The structural validator implements the subset of draft 2020-12 these schemas use, not the whole specification. Mitigated by a differential check against the reference jsonschema implementation, which is an oracle and not a runtime dependency.
